chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so static reverse engineering via MCP is impossible; must reason from symbols, stack, and Java callgraph only.

Top native frame below allocator is byte_array_to_bson_string, called from bson_array_from_bytes / bson_object_from_bytes inside libbson-c-lib.so, with ultimate crash in memmove – classic pattern of buffer overrun or invalid pointer due to incorrect bounds while parsing BSON bytes.

The JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is in the same library; given naming it likely takes a jbyteArray and its length, then passes them into bson_object_from_bytes without additional checks beyond trusting the encoded BSON size fields.

Java-side callgraph shows decodeFromBytes(byte[]) is used to decode arbitrary protocol payloads from SdlPacket.getTag(), which in turn is populated from data coming over Bluetooth / transport from a head unit; that is attacker-controlled relative to the app, and there is no indication of Java-side validation of BSON structure or length consistency.

memmove aborts typically when source/dest overlap incorrectly or when size exceeds object size (checked builds) or hits invalid address; with fuzzing, malformed lengths in BSON (e.g., claimed document length larger than actual buffer) could cause out-of-bounds read/writes inside byte_array_to_bson_string or bson_* functions if they trust length fields without verifying against the actual buffer length passed from JNI.

Because the fuzz harness passes raw byte arrays into the same JNI as production Java would, and the Java callgraph shows realistic paths from remote/IPC input to the decoder, this is not a harness-only bug but a real parsing robustness issue; malformed BSON from a peer could trigger the same memmove-based crash in production, yielding at least a reliable denial-of-service, and potentially memory corruption if not all builds have hardened memmove checks.

Without decompilation we cannot see exact index arithmetic, but the combination of: parsing untrusted binary format, native C implementation, memmove crash, and lack of visible bounds validation strongly indicates an out-of-bounds access (CWE-787/125) driven by attacker-controlled BSON length fields.

Therefore classify as a likely genuine vulnerability, with high but not absolute confidence; severity high because of native memory corruption potential from remotely influenced input via protocol packets.

chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in memmove, called from byte_array_to_bson_string → bson_object_from_bytes → JNI → Java. This is typical of unsafe buffer manipulation based on BSON length fields or array length. Tools are offline so I must infer from symbols and Java callgraph only.

The first app-level native frame below the allocator/sanitizer is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so. Its name strongly suggests it converts a Java byte[]/raw buffer containing BSON into a C string/buffer, likely by allocating/copying based on parsed length fields. Crash at memmove implies out-of-bounds read/write or overlap misuse, nearly always due to incorrect length or bounds validation.

Data-flow / taint reasoning: input is a byte[] passed from Java BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes. Fuzzer drives fuzz_one_input → JNI and provides arbitrary bytes. In normal app operation, those bytes come from SdlPacket.getTag(), which is derived from SDL protocol packets (remote side / vehicle / app). That’s attacker-controlled or at least untrusted remote input. I see no evidence of Java-side validation of BSON structure or bounds before passing to native, only decoding. So native is operating directly on untrusted data.

Because memmove crashed, length/addresses are inconsistent with allocated region. That usually indicates: (1) computed length from BSON header exceeds actual buffer size; (2) negative/overflowed length converted to huge size_t; or (3) derived destination pointer invalid due to misparsed offsets. With no harness-specific anomalies (no obvious NULLs, no impossible states), this is a typical memory safety bug in the BSON C implementation.

Reachability: The Java callgraph paths all start from decodeFromBytes called while handling packets in SdlProtocolBase, WiProProtocol, SdlRouterService, etc., all triggered by remotely supplied SdlPacket instances. So arbitrary peers can send crafted BSON payloads via the protocol to reach this code. That makes it a realistic remote DoS and potentially exploitable memory corruption in the app or hosting process.

We lack decompiled C code, so we cannot nail the exact CWE variant with certainty, but given memmove and a conversion routine, it is almost certainly an out-of-bounds read/write (CWE-787/788) or integer overflow leading to a mis-sized copy (CWE-190/ CWE-131). The vulnerable function is app-level (not libc), and no validation is visible, increasing confidence.

Given this, I classify the crash as a likely genuine vulnerability: attacker-controlled bytes -> native BSON decoder -> unsafe memmove -> crash/corruption. Severity is high because of memory corruption in a network-facing component, though RCE is not proven. Confidence is not absolute (no direct code view) but strong based on stack symbols and context, so ~0.8.

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I must base analysis only on the textual crash entry and general knowledge of BSON parsers and JNI patterns.

The top crashing frame is __memcpy, with the first app frame byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, followed by bson_array_from_bytes and bson_object_from_bytes, then the JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes. This strongly suggests a memcpy inside the BSON decoding path.

Because I cannot decompile functions, I infer data flow from names and Java call graph. JNI method bson_object_from_bytes([B)J takes a Java byte[] that comes from BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes, which is used on SdlPacket.getTag() payloads in various protocol handlers. SdlPacket data ultimately originates from external transport (Bluetooth, USB, network) and is attacker-controlled from the SDL peer side.

There is no indication of size validation in native layer: the native library exposes simple converters (byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_*_from_bytes) that typically interpret an on-wire BSON document whose declared length is read from the first 4 bytes and then used in memcpy to move or assemble elements. Many historical BSON C libs have OOB and length-trust issues when document length is malformed.

The crash in __memcpy usually indicates either invalid destination pointer or an out-of-bounds source/destination due to trusting a length field. In a decoder for untrusted bytes, this is a classic pattern of a memory-safety bug. The fuzz harness simply forwards arbitrary bytes into the same JNI method reachable from live protocol paths, so this is likely a real reachable bug, not a harness artifact (no evidence that fuzz passed impossible parameters like NULL env or invalid JNI array length; Java signature is just [B).

Even without exact C code, there is strong contextual evidence: (1) low-level memcpy crash inside BSON parsing; (2) bytes are attacker-controlled packet fields; (3) no mention of any bounds checking or Java-side length limits beyond normal array bounds; (4) the library is custom libbson-c-lib.so, not hardened system code. This aligns with CWE-119/787 (buffer overflow) or at least CWE-120 (unsafe memcpy).

Exploitability: a memcpy overrun in native parser can lead to heap or stack corruption and potential code execution, depending on layout. At minimum it is a robust native DoS. Given it processes network-ish data in an app component, I rate severity high.

Limitations: I cannot inspect whether there are internal bounds checks or whether memcpy length is capped by the actual JNI array length. It is theoretically possible that the crash is from reading beyond the document into unmapped memory even when using GetByteArrayRegion, but that would still be a decoder bug triggered by attacker-controlled metadata, and many such crashes are exploitable. Absent code, exact CWE and severity are approximate, but classification as vulnerability is still well-supported.

chain_of_thought: Native crash is in __memcpy with preceding frames entirely in libbson-c-lib.so: byte_array_to_bson_string -> bson_array_from_bytes -> bson_object_from_bytes -> JNI wrapper. This is classic pattern of an out-of-bounds memcpy while parsing attacker-controlled bytes into a BSON structure.

I attempted to use Ghidra MCP search_functions_by_name to decompile the relevant functions, but the MCP backend is unavailable (connection refused). Therefore I cannot quote exact decompiled code, but the function names and stack layout are explicit, and __memcpy being the terminator implies length/pointer misuse.

Given the Java call graph, the JNI bridge <com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder: bson_object_from_bytes([B)J> is invoked during parsing of SDL protocol packets, ultimately from SdlRouterService / SdlProtocolBase that handle packets read from external transports (Bluetooth, USB, etc.). These are remote inputs from the head unit or app, so the byte[] passed into BSON decoder is attacker-controlled in realistic scenarios.

The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input directly calls the JNI bridge with arbitrary byte arrays, which mirrors the real use where decodeFromBytes() is handed data from SdlPacket.getTag(). No evidence in the graph indicates any validation of BSON length fields before they are passed to native code; BSON parsers commonly trust embedded length fields (document length, CString termination, array sizes).

The presence of both bson_object_from_bytes and bson_array_from_bytes plus byte_array_to_bson_string suggests a custom BSON implementation. A memcpy-based crash here strongly suggests it is using length values directly from the input buffer (e.g., reading a 32-bit length then memcpy-ing that many bytes into a smaller buffer, or reading a string length and copying without verifying it stays within the total buffer size).

Because the crash happens in __memcpy (not something like abort() or sanitizer), it is a true memory corruption (buffer overflow or invalid read/write), not just an intentional bounds check. memcpy crashing normally means the destination or source pointer is invalid or the size runs off the mapped region; in all these cases, an out-of-bounds access is present and is controlled by input through the BSON format fields.

Given this is a BSON decoder reachable from remotely supplied protocol packets, and there is no indication of constraints on the payload size or thorough validation before calling native decoder, this is a likely exploitable memory corruption vulnerability (potential RCE depending on environment). It is not a mere harness artifact: the Java call graph shows realistic paths where arbitrary remote peers can cause BSON decoding of crafted byte arrays.

Limitations: I cannot decompile the exact functions nor inspect argument values to memcpy, so I cannot pinpoint the exact field (document length vs string length) nor confirm whether certain upper bounds exist. However, fuzzing found a direct crash in memcpy with no JNI/Java guards visible, strongly indicating missing validation in libbson-c-lib's parsing routines.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below libc is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, calling memcpy where crash occurs (__memcpy). Under normal circumstances this memcpy should only copy within allocated buffers; crash implies either src/dst pointer invalid or length too large/out-of-bounds.

I attempted to use Ghidra MCP to decompile byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_object_from_bytes, and the JNI bridge, but the search_functions_by_name calls failed (connection refused), so I cannot inspect exact C code, argument flow, or validations. I therefore reason from function naming, stack, and Java call graph only.

The stack trace shows: Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes -> bson_object_from_bytes -> byte_array_to_bson_string -> __memcpy. This is a straightforward JNI path: a Java byte[] is handed to native BSON decoding, which at some point converts the byte array to a BSON string and uses memcpy. There is no evidence of the fuzzer doing anything impossible (like passing invalid JNI references); it just supplies arbitrary byte[] data to a normal API exposed to the app.

The Java call graph indicates that decodeFromBytes([B) is invoked from multiple protocol handlers (SdlProtocolBase, SdlRouterService, WiProProtocol, etc.) processing SdlPacket data. These handlers ultimately process data coming over a transport (Bluetooth, TCP, etc.). That means the bytes passed into BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes, and hence into the JNI bson_object_from_bytes, are derived from untrusted network/transport input, not from fixed constants.

Given that memcpy crashes, the most plausible explanation is that native code trusts BSON length fields or string sizes encoded in the provided buffer, uses them to compute copy lengths or offsets, and then calls memcpy without robust bounds checking against the actual byte[] length. For malformed or attacker-controlled BSON, this causes out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer dereference, leading to a crash in __memcpy. That pattern is a classic memory corruption bug (CWE-787/119) in parsers that treat length fields as trustworthy.

Because I could not decompile the native functions, I cannot point to the exact line or variable, but the function names (bson_object_from_bytes, byte_array_to_bson_string) strongly indicate parsing and conversion logic that will iterate over the supplied buffer and copy into internal structures. The fuzzer-triggered crash in memcpy is consistent with missing validation of encoded lengths or index arithmetic. Given that the same JNI method is used in real app paths that consume attacker-influenced data, this is not just a harness-only condition; malicious peers could send crafted packets to trigger it.

Impact-wise, an out-of-bounds memcpy in a parser on untrusted data is at minimum a reliable denial-of-service (remote crash) and often leads to exploitable memory corruption depending on heap layout. Without code, I cannot prove full RCE, but the vulnerability is serious and should be treated as high severity until ruled out by code review and tests.

Therefore I classify this as a likely genuine vulnerability: a native BSON decoding routine using memcpy on untrusted, insufficiently validated input length, reachable via network/transport protocol paths. Confidence is somewhat reduced (0.7) due to missing direct code evidence but still solid given context, crash site, and realistic data-flow from untrusted sources.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, so first app-level frame is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so.
Tooling to decompile/search functions is unavailable (connection refused), so I cannot inspect the exact memcpy call sites or do precise backward data-flow within the native code.

However, the crash site (__memcpy) strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer due to an unsafe copy; this is a common pattern in BSON parsers that trust length fields or sizes from the input.

The call chain: Java BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes -> JNI BsonEncoder_bson_object_from_bytes -> native bson_object_from_bytes -> byte_array_to_bson_string -> __memcpy. No indication of any validation layer in Java (decodeFromBytes appears to just forward a byte[]), and BSON is a complex, length-delimited binary format; malformed or attacker-controlled data coming from protocol packets (SdlPacket, SdlProtocolBase, SdlRouterService, etc.) is realistic in production (remote peer traffic).

Given the app context (SmartDeviceLink protocol, router service, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth transport), the BSON bytes are ultimately derived from data sent over a vehicle/mobile link, which can be attacker-controlled or at least remotely influenced. There is no evidence of strict size or structural validation before calling into JNI.

Even without decompilation, typical BSON C libraries implement memcpy-based field extraction using lengths read directly from the buffer (e.g., string_length, BSON document length). If the implementation does not sufficiently bounds-check these against the total buffer size (which is a common bug surface), invalid lengths can cause memcpy to operate past the end or with a bogus pointer, causing this exact crash.

Because the harness simply provides arbitrary byte[] but mirrors a realistic usage path (BSON from SdlPacket tag payloads), the crash is not clearly a harness-only artifact; instead it highlights that malformed BSON from the network/app interface can crash native code.

Thus, this is best categorized as a likely memory-corruption vulnerability (out-of-bounds memcpy) reachable via untrusted input, though exact exploitability (RCE vs DoS) cannot be proven without the native code. At minimum, remote DoS is realistic; with more careful analysis it could escalate to RCE.

Uncertainty remains because I cannot view the specific native code or confirm that Java doesn’t in fact sanitize lengths. But based on the callgraph and typical design of such JNI BSON bindings, those checks are unlikely and no validation is shown in the provided info.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy called by byte_array_to_bson_string/bson_object_from_bytes, consistent with out-of-bounds or invalid-pointer copy based on input-derived sizes.
JNI bridge takes a raw byte[] from Java (BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes) with no visible size or structure validation before entering native BSON parsing logic.

The bytes originate from SdlPacket handling over SmartDeviceLink transports, which are realistically attacker-controlled or remotely influenced, so malformed payloads can hit this code path in production.

evidence.note: Crash at memcpy implies invalid length or pointer from preceding BSON parsing logic.
First app-level frame below __memcpy, likely performing unsafe memcpy using lengths from BSON input.
JNI entry point taking Java byte[] and forwarding it to native BSON parsing without shown validation.

recommendations: Review and decompile bson_object_from_bytes and byte_array_to_bson_string to locate memcpy/memmove calls and ensure all lengths are validated against the overall buffer size and remaining bytes before copying.
Add robust bounds checking on BSON document length fields and string/array sizes, rejecting malformed inputs early and returning an error instead of performing memcpy on untrusted sizes.

Introduce defensive checks at the Java/JNI boundary (e.g., validating total BSON size and structure in Java or a safe wrapper) and consider enabling hardened allocators/sanitizers during testing to catch similar issues.
assumptions: BSON bytes passed from SdlPacket ultimately derive from remote or externally provided protocol data.
Java BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes and related protocol classes do not fully validate BSON structure/lengths before JNI.
The memcpy crash is due to an out-of-bounds access rather than a pure harness artifact such as deliberately passing null pointers.
limitations: Could not decompile or inspect libbson-c-lib.so functions directly due to tooling connection failure; memcpy argument provenance is inferred.
No direct view of Java implementation of BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes or SdlPacket.getTag to confirm absence of validation.
Allocator/sanitizer logs and exact faulting address/size at memcpy are not available, limiting precise classification of the memory error.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string within libbson-c-lib.so; this is the first application-level native frame below the allocator/runtime. Given typical BSON parsing, byte_array_to_bson_string is likely converting a raw BSON byte array into an internal string representation and uses memmove/memcpy based on length fields encoded in the BSON data.

The JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is called directly from com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes([B)J, which is reached via multiple Java call paths that all originate from network/protocol packets (SdlPacket, SdlProtocolBase, SdlRouterService, WiProProtocol). This strongly suggests the input byte[] is ultimately attacker/peer-controlled over the SDL transport (Bluetooth, USB, or network).

The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input simply feeds arbitrary bytes into this same JNI bridge, but the Java call graph shows that in production the BSON decoder is used to parse untrusted messages without obvious prior validation of BSON structure/lengths on the Java side. The presence of a low-level memmove crash indicates an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap miscalculation driven directly by values in the BSON blob (e.g., claimed element/string length larger than actual buffer).

Even without disassembly, a BSON decoder that trusts embedded length fields and passes them as size to memmove without full bounds checking on the overall buffer length is a classic memory corruption pattern (CWE-787 / CWE-119). No evidence of Java- or JNI-side bounds enforcement on the byte[] length or contents is provided; only the array object itself is passed down. The internal C library (libbson-c-lib.so) is generic and will see only the byte pointer and size; if it reads internal length fields and then memmoves based on them, malformed input can trigger crashes like the one observed.

Because libbson is parsing untrusted data from SdlPacket without comprehensive validation at a higher layer, an attacker controlling SDL packets can realistically reach this path. The memmove crash likely corresponds to an out-of-bounds access and therefore constitutes a genuine memory safety vulnerability that could at least be used for denial-of-service and potentially for more severe exploitation depending on allocator/layout and surrounding code. However, without Ghidra output, exact exploitability (RCE vs. DoS) cannot be definitively proven, so severity is marked as high, not critical.

The issue is not attributable to the harness alone: the same parsing routine and memmove call path is clearly reachable from normal Java entry points that process external protocol messages. Therefore this reflects a real vulnerability in BSON parsing logic within libbson-c-lib.so, not an artifact of unrealistic JNI usage.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove invoked by byte_array_to_bson_string within libbson-c-lib.so, consistent with out-of-bounds memory operation during BSON parsing
JNI bridge takes a raw byte[] originating from SdlPacket and passes it directly to native BSON parser without visible structural/length validation in Java
Java call graph shows this path is used for handling external protocol messages, so attacker-controlled data can trigger the same native code path in production

evidence.snippet: Crash in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string while handling BSON byte array

evidence.snippet: Converts BSON-encoded byte array to string using memmove without visible high-level validation

recommendations: Review and harden libbson-c-lib.so BSON parsing routines (byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_array_from_bytes, bson_object_from_bytes) to ensure all length fields are validated against the total buffer size before memmove/memcpy calls
Add robust bounds checks in native code around all memmove/memcpy operations that use values derived from parsed BSON data, returning parse errors instead of proceeding on inconsistent lengths

Consider adding defensive validation in Java before calling the JNI BSON decoder (e.g., maximum size limits, sanity checks) and update to a maintained, security-reviewed BSON implementation if available

memmove crash is due to invalid length or pointer from BSON parsing rather than an unrelated allocator or hardware issue

limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unavailable, so exact C implementation and specific arithmetic/length bug in byte_array_to_bson_string could not be inspected
Jadx MCP could not be used to inspect com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes implementation for any subtle validation it might perform
Exploitability beyond denial-of-service (e.g., achieving code execution) cannot be conclusively demonstrated from the available crash data alone

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator is byte_array_to_bson_string (memmove wrapper). Tools are unavailable so I infer from names and call stack only.

Crash reason is memmove, which usually indicates out-of-bounds or overlapping move with bad length. The surrounding functions (bson_*_from_bytes, byte_array_to_bson_string) are BSON parsers/decoders operating on attacker-controlled byte arrays.

JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes takes a byte[] from Java (BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes). Java call graph shows decodeFromBytes([B) being used on data coming from SdlPacket.getTag() which is derived from SmartDeviceLink protocol packets, i.e., remote/app-supplied network data.

No Java-side length validation is indicated; method name bson_object_from_bytes([B)J suggests the raw byte[] is passed directly. Native bson parsing code typically walks BSON structures and copies fields into C buffers using memmove/memcpy. If the BSON length header or element lengths are malformed, and there is no robust bounds checking, memmove may read/write beyond the provided buffer.

Because the fuzzer (fuzz_one_input) triggered this memmove crash simply by varying the input bytes, and the same path is reachable via real protocol packets, this is a realistic, attacker-controllable memory safety issue, not a harness artifact.

Without decompiled code I cannot pinpoint the exact expression for memmove length, but the pattern (byte_array_to_bson_string → memmove) strongly indicates copying a computed string length from the BSON payload into a buffer sized differently (likely using the BSON-reported length as trust anchor). This is classic CWE-787/119 style integer/length misuse.
Therefore, classify as a genuine, likely exploitable vulnerability with high severity, though I must keep confidence below 0.9 because I lack direct code view.

Pattern matches typical BSON length/field parsing bugs that cause out-of-bounds memcpy/memmove
